I'VE can do something" the en-
gineers said, as they unloaded two
tons of dynamite. mot much.
Maybe nothing at all. The sun is

And II it break all at once"
They shuddered for answer.


Never since the first Jesuit ex-
plorer fell on his knees before the
falls in holy woider-perhaps not
since the first savage boatman,
needless of the sulien warning roar
ahead, discovered too late, and
plunged down to horrible death
and obliviOn-perhaps not since
the Ice Age $4,000 years ago, when
the gorge was first carved from the
sandstone cliffs-had the ice block
ed the passage of the strongest
water power in the world.


1t didn't look dangerous-this
white river of ice.


1t lay like dead thing, motion
less, gray. It's powdered surface
was rough and assured. In some
places were crevasses. extending
deep. In others small mountains
rose above the rest of the surface.
But 1t lay there, inert, inactive.
without movement.


But while l looked spot In the
center began to rise. There was
a creaking and grooming sound
that reached my ears clear up on
the bluff. Something down in that
turgid mass was writhing and
fighting. The uneasy spot formed a
hilloCk, then was still.
